metadata.title: The Meaning of Decentralization Knowledge Graph
metadata.description: Concept dependency graph covering Vitalik's three axes of decentralization, Web3 identity, headless brands, and distributed consensus — 18 nodes, 22 edges
metadata.source: Vitalik Buterin 'The Meaning of Decentralization' (2017), Nichanan Kesonpat 'Web3 Identity Stack' (2022), Other Internet 'Headless Brands' (2019), Preethi Kasireddy 'Distributed Consensus' (2018)

nodes.name: Three Axes of Decentralization
nodes.description: Vitalik Buterin's framework distinguishing architectural, political, and logical decentralization as independent dimensions of system design.
key_ideas: Architectural: How many physical computers comprise the system
Political: How many individuals/organizations control those computers
Logical: Does the system appear as a single monolithic object or an amorphous swarm

nodes.name: Architectural Decentralization
nodes.description: The physical distribution of computers in a system — how many machines exist and how many can fail before the system breaks.
key_ideas: Measures fault tolerance at the hardware level
CDNs are architecturally decentralized but politically centralized
Traditional corporations are architecturally centralized (single HQ)

nodes.name: Political Decentralization
nodes.description: The distribution of control over a system — how many individuals or organizations can make decisions about its operation.
key_ideas: Traditional corporations: CEO = single point of political control
Common law: Judges make independent decisions = politically decentralized
Structural centralization often leads to political centralization, but not always

nodes.name: Logical Decentralization
nodes.description: Whether a system's interface and data structures appear as a single object or a swarm — can the system be split and continue operating independently?
key_ideas: Blockchain is logically centralized: single consensus state
Language is logically decentralized: can split and both halves work
Logical centralization provides convenience (single database)

nodes.name: Fault Tolerance
nodes.description: The ability of a system to continue operating despite component failures — a key reason for pursuing decentralization.
key_ideas: Probability of 5/10 machines failing simultaneously is much lower than 1 machine failing
Common mode failure: All components fail for the same reason (same bug, same manufacturer)
Requires multiple client implementations, geographically distributed developers

nodes.name: Attack Resistance
nodes.description: The cost of attacking a decentralized system increases with the number of independent participants — coercion becomes harder at scale.
key_ideas: 1 person with $50M is easier to coerce than 10 people with $5M each
Smaller targets have better attack/defense cost ratios
PoS > PoW for attack resistance: coins are easier to hide than mining hardware

nodes.name: Collusion Resistance
nodes.description: Preventing unwanted coordination among system participants — distinguishing beneficial cooperation from harmful collusion.
key_ideas: Collusion = coordination we don't want (like antitrust violations)
Selfish mining: 1/3+ hashpower enables unfair advantage
Paradox: How to encourage 'good' cooperation while preventing 'bad' collusion

nodes.name: Common Mode Failure
nodes.description: When multiple components fail for the same reason — a critical vulnerability that decentralization must address.
key_ideas: 4 jet engines from the same factory can all fail if one worker made errors
Same client software bug affecting all nodes
70% of miners in one country = government seizure risk

nodes.name: Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs)
nodes.description: W3C standard for self-sovereign identifiers that don't require central authorities for creation or verification.
key_ideas: Four properties: Decentralized, Persistent, Resolvable, Verifiable
DID Document contains authentication methods and service endpoints
100+ DID methods registered, each with its own Verifiable Data Registry

nodes.name: Web3 Identity Stack
nodes.description: Layered architecture for digital self-sovereignty: blockchain registry → decentralized storage → data composability → authentication.
key_ideas: Layer 1: Blockchain as Verifiable Data Registry
Layer 2: Decentralized storage (IPFS, Arweave, Filecoin)
Layer 3: Data mutability protocols (Ceramic, ComposeDB)

nodes.name: Cryptocurrency as PKI Trojan Horse
nodes.description: Cryptocurrency incentives accelerated public key infrastructure adoption — financial motivation drove key management to mainstream users.
key_ideas: ICO, DeFi, NFT 'carrots' put more keys in user hands than ever before
Losing private key now means losing identity, not just money
Cryptocurrency blurred the line between what we own and who we are

nodes.name: Headless Brands
nodes.description: Brands without central management — when protocol ownership is distributed to users, no single entity controls the narrative.
key_ideas: Brand = consensus system maintaining shared beliefs
Traditional broadcast media gave corporations narrative control
Network media made brands unstable — anyone can publish counter-narratives

nodes.name: Bitcoin: The First Headless Brand
nodes.description: Bitcoin established the archetype of headless branding — strong origin story, anonymous founder, community-evolved narratives.
key_ideas: Satoshi created name and visual identity, then vanished
Brand evolved: e-cash → censorship-resistant store of value → uncorrelated asset
Protocol properties became brand elements: 21M cap, PoW, whitepaper

nodes.name: Narrative Forks and Exit vs Voice
nodes.description: Hard forks as narrative divergence — the tension between allowing exit (fork) and providing voice (governance) in decentralized systems.
key_ideas: Bitcoin Cash fork was primarily a narrative fork
Exit can consolidate remaining narrative (BCH departure ended block size debate)
On-chain governance (Tezos, Decred) internalizes exit as voice

nodes.name: Distributed Consensus Fundamentals
nodes.description: The challenge of achieving agreement across spatially separated computers without a global clock or guaranteed message delivery.
key_ideas: No global clock to order events
Messages can be delayed, duplicated, or dropped
Replicated state machine: multiple computers maintaining identical transaction logs

nodes.name: FLP Impossibility
nodes.description: Fischer-Lynch-Paterson theorem proving that deterministic consensus is impossible in asynchronous systems with even one faulty process.
key_ideas: A single faulty process can prevent consensus at the exact wrong moment
Workarounds: synchrony assumptions (DLS) or non-determinism (PBFT)
Partial synchrony: system eventually becomes synchronous

nodes.name: Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)
nodes.description: Consensus algorithms that tolerate malicious (Byzantine) nodes — requires 2/3+ honest participants.
key_ideas: Byzantine nodes can lie, delay, or send conflicting messages
PBFT: Pre-prepare → Prepare → Commit phases
O(n²) message complexity limits scalability

nodes.name: Nakamoto Consensus
nodes.description: Bitcoin's probabilistic consensus using Proof of Work and longest chain rule — permissionless and Sybil-resistant.
key_ideas: Probabilistic termination: finality increases with confirmations
Sybil resistance: real resource consumption prevents fake nodes
Longest chain rule: 51%+ honest hashpower ensures honest chain wins

edges.description: Architectural decentralization is one of the three axes
Political decentralization is one of the three axes
Logical decentralization is one of the three axes
Physical distribution enables fault tolerance
Distributed control increases cost of attack
Distributed control helps prevent unwanted coordination
Fault tolerance must address common mode failures
Decentralization framework explains headless brand emergence
Bitcoin exemplifies headless brand theory
Bitcoin forks demonstrate narrative divergence
Collusion resistance relates to managing narrative forks
Understanding decentralization is prerequisite for identity stack
DIDs are a core component of the identity stack
Crypto adoption enabled identity stack development
Understanding consensus requires knowing its theoretical limits
BFT algorithms work around FLP impossibility
Nakamoto consensus uses probabilistic approach to circumvent FLP
Fault tolerance in distributed systems requires consensus
Consensus algorithms implement architectural decentralization
Nakamoto consensus is the technical foundation of Bitcoin
Attack resistance analysis applies to consensus mechanisms
BFT provides attack resistance against malicious actors
